title: The Morning Market
introKo:
토요일 아침, 시장에 가요.
할머니가 슬쩍 얹어 주는 덤 한 줌에 마음이 따뜻해져요.

quiz.q: 할머니가 봉지에 덤으로 더 넣어 준 것은 무엇인가요?

easy.text: It is Saturday morning. You walk to the market. The sun is warm.
The market is full of people. You see fruit and vegetables everywhere.

options.reaction: An old grandmother sits behind the stall. She smiles at you.
You see bread, fish, and flowers. But the best smell comes from the vegetable stall. "I saw you walking around," the grandmother laughs. "Everyone comes to me in the end."

easy.text: The grandmother has eggplants, melons, and green onions. Everything looks fresh.
"Good morning," she says. "Everything is from my garden."

options.reaction: "Three for five dollars," she says. "They are the best in the market."
"Of course!" she laughs. "I picked them this morning."

easy.text: You choose three eggplants and one big melon.
"That is nine dollars," she says. "Can you give me a little discount?" you ask. The grandmother laughs. "Okay, okay. One dollar off, just for you."
She takes your money. Then she puts an extra handful of green onions into your bag. "A little extra for you," she says.

options.en: Thank you! You are so kind.
options.reaction: "Come again," she says. "I am here every Saturday."

options.en: I will come back next week!
options.reaction: "Good!" she laughs. "I will keep the best melons for you."

easy.text: You walk home with a heavy bag and a light heart. In the bag, the green onions smell fresh. Next Saturday, you will come back.

normal.text: It is Saturday morning, and you walk to the town market. The sun feels warm on your face.
The market is already busy, and you can hear the sellers calling out their prices. Baskets of fruit and vegetables fill every corner.

options.en: Go straight to the vegetable stall.
options.reaction: An old grandmother sits behind the vegetable stall. When she sees you, she smiles warmly.

options.en: Take a walk around the market first.
options.reaction: You walk past stalls of bread, fish, and flowers, but the best smell pulls you to the vegetable stall. "I saw you walking around," the grandmother laughs. "Everyone comes to me in the end."

normal.text: The grandmother sells eggplants, melons, and green onions, and everything on her stall still looks fresh from the morning.
"Good morning, dear," she says with a smile. "Everything here comes from my own garden."

options.reaction: "Three for five dollars," she answers with pride. "Nobody in this market sells better ones."

options.en: Wow, everything here looks really fresh!
options.reaction: "Of course it is!" she laughs. "I picked all of them myself early this morning."

normal.text: You choose three eggplants and one round melon, and the melon already smells sweet.
"That is nine dollars together," she says. "Can you give me a small discount?" you ask carefully. The grandmother laughs out loud. "Okay, one dollar off, but only because I like your face."
She takes your money and then puts an extra handful of green onions into your bag. "A little extra, just for you," she says softly.

options.en: Thank you so much! You are really kind.
options.reaction: "Come again, dear," she says. "I am here every Saturday morning."

options.en: I will come back again next week!
options.reaction: "Good!" she laughs. "Then I will keep my best melons just for you."

normal.text: You walk home with a heavy bag and a light heart. The morning market gave you more than vegetables today.

hard.text: It is a bright Saturday morning, and you make your way to the town market, where the smell of fresh fruit drifts through the air.
The market is crowded and full of cheerful noise, with stalls of fruit and vegetables stretching in every direction.

options.en: Head straight to the vegetable stall.
options.reaction: An old grandmother sits behind the vegetable stall, arranging her eggplants with care. When she notices you, her face breaks into a warm smile.

You wander past stalls of bread, fresh fish, and flowers, but the finest smell of all draws you back to the vegetable stall. "I watched you circle the whole market," the grandmother chuckles. "They always end up at my stall."

hard.text: The grandmother sells shiny eggplants, round melons, and bundles of green onions, all of which look freshly picked.
"Good morning, dear," she says warmly. "Everything on this table comes from my own garden, watered by these two hands."

options.reaction: "Three for five dollars," she announces with pride. "You will not find better ones in this whole market."
"Of course it does!" she laughs. "I picked every single one of them myself before the sun rose."

hard.text: You pick out three eggplants and a round melon that smells sweet even before you lift it.

"That comes to nine dollars," she says. "Any chance of a small discount?" you ask, half joking. The grandmother throws her head back and laughs. "Fine, one dollar off, but only because you have an honest face."
She takes your money, and then, without a word, she drops an extra handful of green onions into your bag. "A little something extra, just for you," she murmurs.

options.en: Thank you! You are so kind.
options.reaction: "Come again anytime, dear," she says. "You will find me here every Saturday morning, rain or shine."

options.en: I will definitely come back next week!
options.reaction: "Good!" she laughs. "Then I will set aside my very best melons just for you."

hard.text: You walk home with a heavy bag and a light heart, and it strikes you that the morning market gave you far more than vegetables today.

aside.ko: 따로, 한쪽에 (set aside: 따로 남겨 두다)

chance.ko: 가능성 (any chance of: 혹시 ~ 안 될까요)

end.ko: 끝 (in the end: 결국 / end up: 결국 ~하게 되다)

extra.ko: 여분의, 덤으로 주는 (a little extra: 덤)

or.ko: 또는 (rain or shine: 비가 오나 해가 뜨나)

shine.ko: 맑은 날씨 (rain or shine: 비가 오나 해가 뜨나)

something.ko: 무언가 (a little something: 작은 것 하나)

throws.ko: 젖히다, 던지다 (throw one's head back: 고개를 젖히다)

introEn:
Saturday morning at the town market.
A little extra from grandma warms your heart.
